meta.title: Code Review Meta
meta.description: Meta checklist for reviewing the review itself: PR quality, commit hygiene, test coverage, and review process

items.text: Is the PR a reasonable size (< 400 lines of meaningful changes)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: PRs should be small enough to review thoroughly. Large PRs get rubber-stamped because reviewers lose focus.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Studies show review quality drops sharply after 200-400 lines. Large PRs hide bugs in the volume of changes.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check the total lines changed. Exclude auto-generated files, lockfiles, and migrations from the count. If over 400 meaningful lines, consider splitting.
baseContent.exampleComment: This PR changes 1200 lines across 30 files. Consider splitting into smaller, independently reviewable PRs.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Keep PRs under 400 meaningful lines. Split large changes into a stack of smaller PRs.

items.text: Does the PR have a single, clear purpose?

baseContent.whatItMeans: A PR should do one thing: fix a bug, add a feature, or refactor code. Don't mix unrelated changes.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Multi-purpose PRs are hard to review, hard to revert, and hard to bisect when bugs are found later.
baseContent.howToVerify: Read the PR description. Can the purpose be stated in one sentence? Are there changes unrelated to that purpose?
baseContent.exampleComment: This PR adds a new endpoint AND refactors the auth middleware AND fixes a typo in docs. Split these into 3 separate PRs.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: One PR, one purpose. Separate refactoring from feature work.

items.text: Does the PR have a clear description explaining what and why?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The PR description should explain what changed, why, any trade-offs made, and how to test it.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without context, reviewers waste time understanding the motivation. Future developers can't understand historical decisions.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check the PR description. Does it explain the motivation? Is there a test plan? Are trade-offs documented?
baseContent.exampleComment: The PR description is empty. Please add what problem this solves, the approach taken, and how to test it.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Write PR descriptions for the reviewer and for future developers. Explain why, not just what.

items.text: Is the PR linked to a ticket/issue for traceability?

baseContent.whatItMeans: PRs should reference the ticket or issue they address. Use 'Closes #123' or link to the Jira/Linear ticket.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unlinked PRs make it impossible to trace why a change was made. This matters during debugging and audits.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check the PR description for a ticket reference. Verify the ticket exists and the PR addresses it.
baseContent.exampleComment: Which ticket does this PR address? Please add a reference (e.g., Closes #123) for traceability.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Link PRs to tickets. It enables traceability and automatic ticket updates.

items.text: Are commit messages meaningful and descriptive?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Commit messages should describe what changed and why. Use conventional commits or the project's commit convention.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Good commit messages enable git blame/log investigations. 'fix stuff' or 'wip' messages provide no useful context.
baseContent.howToVerify: Read the commit messages. Do they explain the change? Would they help someone debugging this code in 6 months?
baseContent.exampleComment: Commit messages like 'fix' and 'update' are not helpful. Please rewrite to describe what was fixed and why.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Write commit messages for future developers. Describe the why, not just the what.

items.text: Are commits logically organized (not 'fix typo' after 'add feature')?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Each commit should be a logical unit of work. Fixup commits should be squashed. The commit history should tell a story.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A clean commit history enables git bisect, makes reverts easier, and helps reviewers understand the progression.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if commits build on each other logically. Look for fixup/WIP commits that should be squashed before merge.
baseContent.exampleComment: There are 3 'fix typo' commits after the feature commit. Squash these into the original commit before merging.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Organize commits logically. Squash fixup commits before merging.

items.text: Are there no unrelated changes snuck into commits?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Commits should only contain changes related to their message. Unrelated formatting, refactoring, or fixes should be separate.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unrelated changes in commits make reverts risky (reverting the feature also reverts the unrelated fix) and obscure git blame.
baseContent.howToVerify: Review each commit's diff. Check if all changes relate to the commit message.
baseContent.exampleComment: This commit 'add user endpoint' also reformats 5 unrelated files. Move the formatting to a separate commit.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Keep commits focused. Separate unrelated changes into their own commits or PRs.

items.text: Are new features and bug fixes accompanied by tests?

baseContent.whatItMeans: New features should have tests proving they work. Bug fixes should have tests proving the bug is fixed and preventing regression.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Code without tests will break silently in the future. Bug fixes without tests will regress when the code is changed.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for new/modified test files. Verify tests cover the happy path, error cases, and edge cases.
baseContent.exampleComment: This adds a new payment processing function with no tests. Please add tests covering successful payment, declined card, and network errors.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every feature needs tests. Every bug fix needs a regression test.

items.text: Do tests actually test meaningful behavior (not just coverage)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Tests should assert on behavior and outcomes, not implementation details. A test that passes regardless of bugs is useless.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Tests that only check 'no error thrown' or mock everything provide false confidence. They pass even when the code is broken.
baseContent.howToVerify: Read the test assertions. Would the test fail if the feature was broken? Does it test the actual behavior users care about?
baseContent.exampleComment: This test calls the function but only asserts it doesn't throw. Add assertions on the return value and side effects.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Tests should fail when features break. Assert on behavior and outcomes, not implementation.

items.text: Are edge cases and error paths tested?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Tests should cover boundary conditions: empty inputs, null values, maximum sizes, concurrent access, and error responses.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Most bugs live in edge cases. Happy path tests only catch the most obvious failures.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for tests with empty lists, null/undefined, very large inputs, duplicate entries, and error responses.
baseContent.exampleComment: Tests only cover the happy path. What happens with an empty list? A null user? A duplicate email? Add edge case tests.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test edge cases: empty, null, max, duplicate, concurrent, and error scenarios.

items.text: Does CI pass (all tests, lint, build)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: All CI checks must pass before the PR is approved. Don't approve PRs with failing CI.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Approving PRs with failing CI normalizes broken builds. It's the most basic quality gate.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check the CI status on the PR. All required checks should show green. If CI is flaky, fix the flaky test.
baseContent.exampleComment: CI is failing on this PR. Please fix the failing tests before requesting review.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never approve a PR with failing CI. Fix tests, don't skip them.

items.text: Do you understand the full context before leaving comments?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Read the PR description, linked tickets, and the full diff before commenting. Understand the constraints and trade-offs.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Comments without context waste everyone's time. Suggesting a change that was already considered and rejected creates friction.
baseContent.howToVerify: Before commenting, ask yourself: Did I read the PR description? Do I understand why this approach was chosen?
baseContent.exampleComment: I see this uses approach X instead of Y. Was Y considered? The PR description doesn't mention the trade-off.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Understand the context before commenting. Read the description and linked tickets first.

items.text: Are review comments labeled by severity (blocker, suggestion, nit)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Prefix comments with severity: 'blocker:', 'suggestion:', 'nit:', 'question:'. This helps authors prioritize.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without severity labels, authors don't know which comments must be addressed vs which are optional suggestions.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if your comments clearly indicate whether they block approval or are optional improvements.
baseContent.exampleComment: nit: consider renaming this variable for clarity (non-blocking).
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Label comment severity. Make it clear what blocks approval vs what's a nice-to-have.

items.text: Are comments constructive (suggesting solutions, not just pointing out problems)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Don't just say 'this is wrong'. Explain why and suggest an alternative. Share knowledge, don't gatekeep.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unhelpful comments create friction and slow development. Constructive reviews teach and build team capability.
baseContent.howToVerify: Read your comments from the author's perspective. Would you know how to address each comment? Is the tone respectful?
baseContent.exampleComment: Instead of 'This is wrong', try: 'This could cause X because of Y. Consider using Z approach instead — here's an example: ...'
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Be constructive. Suggest solutions, explain reasoning, and share knowledge.

items.text: Are you reviewing the code objectively (not the person)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Review code on its merits regardless of who wrote it. Don't hold junior devs to different standards or rubber-stamp senior devs.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Biased reviews miss bugs in senior code and discourage junior developers. Everyone's code deserves equal scrutiny.
baseContent.howToVerify: Ask yourself: Would I make the same comment if someone else wrote this code? Am I being more/less critical based on the author?
baseContent.exampleComment: This is a good practice to apply consistently — the same quality standards should apply regardless of who authored the code.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Review code, not people. Apply consistent standards regardless of author seniority.

items.text: Are breaking changes documented and communicated?

baseContent.whatItMeans: API changes, schema changes, config changes, and removed features must be documented in the PR and changelog.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Undocumented breaking changes silently break dependent teams/services. Migration pain is multiplied by every consumer.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if the PR changes any public API, config format, or database schema. Verify breaking changes are called out in the description.
baseContent.exampleComment: This renames the config key from 'db_url' to 'database_url'. This is a breaking change — document it and provide migration instructions.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Document all breaking changes. Provide migration instructions for consumers.

items.text: Are relevant docs, comments, and README updated with the code change?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Code changes that affect behavior should update corresponding documentation, API docs, and inline comments.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Outdated documentation is worse than no documentation. It actively misleads developers and users.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if the changed code has corresponding documentation. Verify docs, README, and API specs are updated.
baseContent.exampleComment: The README still references the old configuration format. Please update it to match the new structure.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Update docs with code changes. Outdated docs are worse than no docs.

items.text: Are deprecated features properly marked and scheduled for removal?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Deprecated features should be marked with @deprecated annotations, documented in the changelog, and have a planned removal date.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Undocumented deprecation surprises users. No removal timeline means deprecated code lingers forever, increasing maintenance burden.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for deprecated code without @deprecated annotations. Verify the deprecation notice includes what to use instead and when it will be removed.
baseContent.exampleComment: This function is being replaced but has no @deprecated annotation. Add one with the alternative and planned removal version.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Mark deprecations clearly. Include the alternative and planned removal timeline.
